points.text: COVID-19 spreads mainly by inhaling droplets or aerosols that contain SARS‑CoV‑2 from infected people. [doc1_covid19.txt:chunk_00024]
Infected individuals release these particles when they breathe, talk, cough, sneeze, or sing. [doc1_covid19.txt:chunk_00024]
Transmission is highest at close range but can occur over longer indoor distances as small particles stay airborne. [doc1_covid19.txt:chunk_00004]
The virus can enter the body through the eyes, nose, or mouth when infectious particles are breathed in. [doc1_covid19.txt:chunk_00004]
Touching contaminated surfaces and then touching the face (eyes, nose, mouth) can also spread COVID‑19. [doc1_covid19.txt:chunk_00004]
People remain contagious for up to 20 days and can transmit the virus even without symptoms. [doc1_covid19.txt:chunk_00004]
COVID-19 spreads mainly person-to-person through respiratory droplets when an infected person coughs, sneezes, breathes, sings or talks. [doc2_covid19.txt:chunk
Droplets can land on a nearby person’s face or be inhaled by people close together, especially in low‑airflow settings. [doc2_covid19.txt:chunk_00008]
The virus travels in tiny airborne droplets that remain suspended and spread through the air in close contact situations. [doc2_covid19.txt:chunk_00001]
Asymptomatic or pre‑symptomatic individuals can transmit the virus, making close contact a key risk factor. [doc2_covid19.txt:chunk_00007]
Risk increases with prolonged exposure (>30 min), crowded indoor spaces, and poor ventilation. [doc2_covid19.txt:chunk_00011]
COVID-19 spreads when an infected person releases droplets or aerosols from the mouth or nose while coughing, sneezing, speaking, singing, or breathing. [doc3_c
Both large respiratory droplets and smaller aerosol particles can transmit the virus, and infected individuals can be contagious even without symptoms. [doc3_co
Touching surfaces contaminated with the virus and then touching the mouth, nose, or eyes can also cause infection. [doc3_covid19.txt:chunk_00005]
